diagnosis_framework.description: A generic problem-diagnosis framework for agricultural and ecological production systems in an Indian tehsil context. Organized by production system > observed stress > causal pathway > variables required for diagnosis. Variables reference the data dictionary; those marked NOT_AVAILABLE are recommended additions. This framework is designed to be aquifer-agnostic, terrain-agnostic, and applicable across drought-prone and well-watered regions.

water_scarcity.description: Farmers report insufficient water for crop production, particularly for rabi and zaid seasons or during dry spells in kharif.

groundwater_stress.description: Declining or insufficient groundwater availability due to overextraction, poor recharge, or aquifer limitations.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Indicates the stage of groundwater extraction at block level; high values (>70%) indicate critical or over-exploited conditions
CGWB categorical classification (Safe / Semi-critical / Critical / Over-exploited)
Increasing well depth over years indicates declining groundwater table; user-supplied when CoRE data unavailable
Linear trend in annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff); sustained negative trend confirms depletion
Annual groundwater recharge; negative values across multiple years signal chronic depletion
Hard rock aquifers (basalt, granite) have low storativity and are more vulnerable to depletion than alluvial aquifers
Lithological mix determines aquifer recharge potential; alluvial % indicates better recharge capacity
High borewell density per unit area signals extraction pressure exceeding recharge
Saline groundwater (common in hard rock and coastal areas) makes available water unusable for irrigation
Cumulative SWC works are a proxy for recharge infrastructure; low counts suggest underinvestment in recharge
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).

solutions: Panchayat-NGO partnership for collective groundwater governance
MGNREGA-funded soil and water conservation (SWC) works with detailed project reports
Trench and check-dam construction for in-situ recharge
Gabion structures on drainage lines to slow runoff
Participatory hydrological mapping to identify recharge zones
Community pond repair and desilting
case_study_examples: Plantation + check dams + farm bunds + bamboo groves
Recharge shafts + percolation ponds + solar lift irrigation

drought.description: Meteorological or agricultural drought reducing water availability during the cropping season.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Count of kharif weeks under severe drought conditions; persistent severe weeks directly cause crop failure
Moderate drought weeks indicate stress periods
Number of weeks under defined dry spell (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall); long dry spells disrupt crop growth stages
SPI indicates meteorological drought severity; MAI measures soil moisture adequacy; VCI captures vegetation stress; together they classify drought type and severity per India Drought Manual
Annual rainfall quantum and inter-annual variability; below-normal years confirm drought risk
Seasonal breakdown reveals whether deficit is in kharif (monsoon) or rabi (post-monsoon irrigation dependent)
Delayed onset shortens effective kharif season and increases crop failure risk
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).

solutions: Check dam repair using community labour ahead of monsoon
Emergency pre-monsoon water harvesting structure construction
Drought-tolerant variety promotion for kharif
case_study_examples: Pond construction + community management + livestock corridor
Bund repair + irrigation improvement + recharge promotion

rainfed_risk.description: High dependence on rainfall with minimal irrigation infrastructure makes agriculture vulnerable to erratic monsoons.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Large area under single kharif cropping indicates rainfed dependence; no rabi cropping without irrigation
Low double-cropped area relative to total cropland confirms limited irrigation access
Low cropping intensity (near 1.0) implies single season rainfed farming; higher values indicate irrigation
Low SWB area persisting into rabi suggests limited surface water storage for post-monsoon irrigation
Presence of a canal indicates access to surface irrigation; absence confirms rainfed dependency
Low count of MGNREGA irrigation works suggests poor irrigation infrastructure investment
Net irrigated area directly quantifies irrigation access; low values confirm rainfed risk
High runoff relative to rainfall indicates rapid water loss; potential for in-situ water harvesting
Sloping terrain increases runoff loss; plain terrain is more amenable to water harvesting
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).

solutions: In-situ water harvesting (farm ponds, bunds, broad-bed furrows)
Promotion of short-duration drought-tolerant crop varieties
Rainwater harvesting structures scaled to slope and drainage density
Crop insurance and risk mitigation advisory

irrigation_challenges.description: Irrigation infrastructure is present but insufficient, costly, or seasonally fails.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Trend in total SWB area over years indicates whether storage infrastructure is expanding or contracting
SWB area in kharif vs rabi vs zaid shows persistence of water storage; rapid drying indicates siltation or poor design
Number of surface water bodies; low count relative to MWS area indicates infrastructure gap
Deep wells imply high lifting costs, disadvantaging marginal farmers; user-supplied when CoRE data unavailable
Presence of a named river can indicate potential for diversion or lift irrigation
Cumulative SWC and irrigation works indicate historical investment in water harvesting infrastructure
Distance to nearest agricultural cooperative society (PACS/marketing co-op) relates to access to institutional credit for irrigation investment
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Visible siltation confirms storage loss driving surface irrigation failure

solutions: Tank desilting and repair through MGNREGA
Micro-irrigation promotion (drip/sprinkler) to improve water use efficiency
Community-managed surface water storage structures
Solar pump installation for groundwater lift irrigation

low_yield.description: Farmers observe declining or stagnant crop yields over years despite similar inputs.

soil_degradation.description: Physical, chemical, or biological deterioration of soil reducing its productive capacity.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Area of farmland transitioning to barren or scrubland is a direct indicator of soil degradation at landscape scale
Farm-to-barren transition specifically indicates severe and likely irreversible soil degradation
Increase in shrub/scrub over time may indicate cropland abandonment due to soil decline
Sloping terrain with high hill_slope_area_percent is more vulnerable to runoff-induced topsoil loss
Higher slopy area indicates greater erosion risk and topsoil loss
Count of land restoration works indicates remediation investment; low counts suggest neglected degraded land
SOC directly measures soil fertility and biological health; declining SOC is the primary marker of soil degradation
Quantifies soil loss; high erosion rates confirm physical degradation even when barren transition is not yet visible
Shallow Vertisols or Entisols on slopes are inherently more vulnerable to erosion; Vertisols in plains can have waterlogging issues

solutions: Organic farming transition including composting, vermicompost, green manure
System of Crop Intensification (SCI/SRI) to improve soil biological activity
Community training on soil health management
Contour bunding and field bunds on slopes to reduce erosion
MGNREGA land restoration works on degraded fields
case_study_examples: Organic seed distribution + training + market linkage

monocropping.description: Continuous cultivation of the same crop depletes specific soil nutrients, increases pest pressure, and reduces resilience.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Area under dominant crops (e.g. cotton monoculture in Vidarbha) directly measures monocrop dependency
Low and stable cropping intensity may indicate seasonal monoculture with no diversification into other seasons
Dominance of single kharif area and low double/triple crop area suggests monoculture cropping pattern
Positive change toward double cropping indicates diversification; negative trend or stagnation confirms continued monocropping
Presence of NGOs working on cultivation practices or agroforestry may indicate ongoing diversification efforts

solutions: Crop diversification demonstrations and farmer field schools
Intercropping and mixed cropping promotion (e.g. soybean-pigeonpea)
Market linkage for diversified crops to make diversification economically viable
Seed support for diverse local and improved varieties

seed_unavailability.description: Farmers cannot access quality seeds of preferred or resilient varieties, including traditional/indigenous varieties.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: NGO presence in seed management domain indicates whether seed systems support is active in the MWS
Distance to nearest agricultural cooperative society (PACS/marketing co-op) is a proxy for access to institutional seed supply systems
Distance to agricultural markets relates to availability of commercial seed supply
Dominance of commercial hybrid crops (e.g. Bt cotton) at expense of traditional varieties indicates reduced seed diversity

solutions: Community seed banks for local variety conservation and distribution
Seed exchange fairs and farmer-to-farmer sharing networks
Integration with government seed distribution schemes
NGO support for indigenous variety documentation and multiplication
case_study_examples: Organic seed distribution + training + market linkage

chemical_dependence.description: Over-reliance on chemical fertilisers and pesticides leading to input cost escalation, soil health decline, and pest resistance.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Declining SOC is the primary measurable consequence of long-term chemical input use and tillage
Proximity to agri-input markets facilitates chemical input access; high access can promote overuse
NGO presence in organic/natural farming or soil conservation domains indicates counter-efforts
Crops like cotton are chemically intensive; higher cotton area implies higher pesticide exposure risk

solutions: Natural/zero-budget natural farming (ZBNF) training and handholding
Biofertiliser and biopesticide production and distribution at village level
Gradual input substitution plans with farmer support groups
Demonstration plots comparing chemical vs natural inputs

crop_failure.description: Partial or total loss of standing crops in a given season.

soil_degradation.description: See low_yield > soil_degradation — same variables apply, with higher severity thresholds expected.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Severe barrenisation indicates areas prone to crop failure
Very low SOC leads to poor water retention and nutrient availability causing crop failure
Severe drought weeks in tandem with degraded soils dramatically increase failure probability

solutions: Emergency soil rehabilitation: contour trenching, vegetative cover
Crop insurance and ex-post relief mechanisms
Shift to perennial or agroforestry systems on severely degraded land

monocropping.description: Monoculture increases susceptibility to pest/disease outbreaks causing sudden crop losses.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Single crop dominance across MWS creates landscape-level vulnerability to pest outbreaks
VCI score captures vegetation condition stress which can reflect crop disease or pest damage at landscape scale

seed_unavailability.description: Use of poor quality or unadapted seeds increases failure risk particularly under stress.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Year-on-year decline in kharif cropped area may indicate farmers abandoning crops due to seed or input failures
Seed management NGO presence can mitigate seed-related failure risk

chemical_dependence.description: Pesticide resistance and input failure contribute to crop loss episodes.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Combined stress from moderate drought and pest resistance is a common failure trigger
Depleted SOC amplifies crop failure risk under input failure

market_access_gap.description: Farmers receive poor prices due to remoteness from markets, lack of storage, or monopolistic intermediaries.

infrastructure_gap.description: Physical distance or absence of market infrastructure prevents remunerative price realisation.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Distance to APMC determines access to regulated market pricing; >20 km severely constrains access
Distance to nearest agricultural trading facility
Absence of nearby cold storage forces distress selling of perishables
Distance to processing units; absence prevents value addition
Agricultural cooperative societies provide collective bargaining and marketing; high distance indicates weak institutional marketing access
Lack of banking access forces reliance on moneylenders and distress sales
Highly perishable crops (vegetables, fruits) with no nearby processing suffer worse post-harvest losses
Direct measure of price realisation gap vs MSP or wholesale price
Marginal/small farmers have insufficient marketable surplus to negotiate prices independently

solutions: Farmer Producer Organisation (FPO) formation for collective marketing
Primary processing unit establishment near the MWS cluster
Cold storage and warehouse linkage through cooperative networks
Digital market platform access (e-NAM, commodity apps) training
APMC sub-yard or direct market infrastructure advocacy
case_study_examples: Organic seed distribution + training + market linkage

livestock_decline.description: Decline in livestock population, condition, or productivity; high mortality; distress sale or migration of animals.

overgrazing.description: Livestock numbers exceed carrying capacity of available grazing land, degrading pastures and causing fodder shortage.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Size of community grazing land relative to livestock population determines stocking density and overgrazing risk
High shrub/scrub area with trend toward barrenisation indicates degraded grazing commons
Increasing barren land area reflects overgrazing-induced pasture degradation
Farm and pasture degradation trend confirms landscape-level overuse
Absolute livestock numbers are essential to compute stocking rate vs carrying capacity
MGNREGA land restoration works include pasture development; low count signals neglect of commons

solutions: Community grazing management plans with seasonal rotational grazing
Village pasture (gairan) development under MGNREGA
Stocking density norms through gram panchayat bylaws
Promotion of stall-fed livestock to reduce grazing pressure

fodder_shortage.description: Insufficient availability of green or dry fodder, especially in rabi and summer seasons.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Area under dedicated fodder crops directly measures fodder production capacity
Water availability in rabi determines ability to grow irrigated fodder crops post-monsoon
Tree cover provides tree fodder (lopping); declining forest area reduces dry season fodder supply
Prolonged dry spells during kharif reduce green fodder availability from crop residue and grasslands
Forest loss reduces tree-fodder availability, intensifying dry-season fodder crisis
Required to compute fodder demand vs available supply

solutions: Silage making and fodder conservation training for dry season
Azolla and multi-cut fodder crop promotion on irrigated plots
Tree fodder species (Subabul, Gliricidia) planting on farm bunds
Community fodder banks with seasonal stocking

pasture_degradation.description: Community grazing lands and grasslands degraded through encroachment, invasives, or neglect.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Available common land area is the primary indicator of pasture extent
Degradation transitions include commons degradation which appears as farm/scrub transitions
Increasing barren land on commons and pasture interfaces signals grazing land degradation
Lantana, Prosopis, and other invasives replace palatable grasses on commons, directly reducing grazing value
Plantation works can include pasture development; trend shows investment in commons rehabilitation
Forest encroachment often co-occurs with commons encroachment

solutions: Common land treatment: grass seeding, gully plugging, protection
Removal of invasive species (Prosopis, Lantana) from commons
MGNREGA pasture development works on degraded gairan
Community bylaws restricting encroachment on commons

water_scarcity_livestock.description: Livestock face water stress particularly in summer months when surface water dries up.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Low zaid SWB area means water bodies dry up before summer ends, directly causing livestock water stress
Low number of water bodies limits spatial access for livestock
Deep wells with high lifting cost are inaccessible to smallholder livestock keepers for animal watering; user-supplied
Severe drought periods accelerate surface water depletion available for livestock
Check dams, ponds, and nalas built under SWC contribute to livestock water availability

solutions: Construction of livestock ponds in grazing areas
Check dam construction to sustain surface water for longer
Cattle troughs at existing water bodies

skip_when.message: Tree cover is below 20% of MWS area — NTFP/forestry pathways skipped.

ntfp_decline.description: Non-timber forest produce collection declining in quantity or quality; reduced access for dependent communities.

forest_degradation.description: Degradation of forest cover reduces NTFP-bearing trees and understory species.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Absolute forest area and its trend over 2017–2024 measures forest cover change
Latest tree/forest cover as percent of MWS area; used for production-system eligibility (skip NTFP when below 10%)
Total deforested area quantifies cumulative habitat loss
Low afforestation relative to deforestation indicates weak forest protection and recovery
Direct survey of NTFP species abundance is the most diagnostic indicator
NGO presence in NTFP or agroforestry domains indicates active NTFP support
Recurring or intensifying fires are a primary degradation driver for NTFP species
Active JFM/CFR/VSS governance reduces ongoing degradation risk
Fragmentation amplifies edge effects and species loss in degraded patches

solutions: Community plantation of indigenous NTFP-bearing species
Fire line creation and community forest watch patrols
Village-level forest protection bylaws and penalties
Joint Forest Management (JFM) or Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights under FRA
Assisted natural regeneration in degraded forest patches
case_study_examples: Forest patrolling + plantation + fire line creation
Water harvesting + afforestation + check dams + community monitoring

encroachment.description: Forest land encroached for agriculture or settlement, reducing forest extent and community access.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Forest-to-farm transition directly measures encroachment
Settlement expansion into forest areas can indicate encroachment
High ST population with declining NTFP access may indicate forest rights disputes or displacement
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Pending or rejected FRA claims indicate tenure insecurity amplifying encroachment impacts
Absent or disputed boundaries precondition encroachment and access loss
Reported fragmentation corroborates encroachment-driven NTFP access loss
Community-reported NTFP decline attributed to area loss or blocked access

solutions: Forest Rights Act (FRA) claim filing support for individual and community rights
Boundary demarcation and signage of forest land
Livelihood alternatives for encroacher households to reduce pressure

invasive_species.description: Invasive plants (Lantana, Prosopis, Parthenium) or aquatic weeds outcompete native NTFP species.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Direct measure of area under invasive species infestation
Increase in shrub/scrub class can partly reflect invasive species expansion into degraded forest edges
Plantation works can include invasive removal and native species replacement; low counts suggest neglect

solutions: Community-led Prosopis/Lantana removal campaigns with product utilisation (charcoal, crafts)
Replacement planting with native species after invasive removal
Aquatic weed management in tanks through community labour

forest_fire.description: Recurring forest fires damage mature trees, understory NTFP species, and regeneration.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Direct count of fire events within MWS per year
Severe drought weeks dry out forest understory increasing fire risk
Long dry spells in kharif or post-kharif elevate fire ignition risk
Dense dry scrubland is highly fire-prone; increase after fire may indicate recurring events

solutions: Fire line creation and annual maintenance before summer
Community fire watch squads with early warning protocols
Fire-resilient species selection in plantations
Controlled burning protocols to reduce dry fuel load

genetic_erosion.description: Loss of indigenous crop varieties, tree species, and medicinal plant diversity from landscapes and seed systems.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Species richness survey is the primary diagnostic for genetic/biodiversity erosion
Replacement of traditional varieties by hybrids in the crop landscape reflects genetic erosion
NGO work in seed management domain indicates active efforts against genetic erosion
Indigenous communities (ST) are primary custodians of traditional seed and plant knowledge; their displacement correlates with genetic erosion

solutions: Community seed banks for indigenous crop and plant varieties
Documentation of traditional ecological knowledge with village elders
Ex-situ conservation nurseries for rare NTFP species
Integration with national PGR (Plant Genetic Resources) networks

wildlife_conflict.description: Damage to crops or livestock by wildlife; increased human-wildlife interactions due to habitat compression.

habitat_loss_fragmentation.description: Reduction and fragmentation of wildlife habitat forces animals into agricultural areas.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Forest loss drives wildlife into farms; high deforestation intensifies conflict
Absolute forest cover and fragmentation trend determines habitat availability
Forest-to-farm conversion is the primary driver of habitat encroachment
Higher-order stream corridors are wildlife movement pathways; their degree of agricultural adjacency affects conflict likelihood

biodiversity_loss.description: Decline in plant and animal species richness and abundance within MWS landscapes.

low_fish_productivity.description: Water bodies within MWS produce low fish yields; declining fish species diversity; inequitable access to fishery resources.

water_potential.description: Tanks and water bodies are under-utilised for fisheries due to seasonal drying, siltation, poor management, or access restrictions.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Total water body area sets the upper limit for fishery potential within MWS
Persistence of water through rabi season determines effective fish growing period; only krz_water supports year-round fisheries
Zaid-persisting water bodies can support one complete fish production cycle
Perennial water bodies (kharif+rabi+zaid) are the only ones suitable for sustained fish production
Number of distinct water bodies; multiple smaller tanks vs fewer large ones affects fishery management
Groundwater recharge balance affects baseflow sustaining perennial water bodies
Actual fish yield data is essential to establish productivity gap vs water body potential
Aquatic NTFP and fish species inventories reveal biodiversity decline in water bodies
MGNREGA community assets include tank desilting and fishery infrastructure; investment trend indicates support
NGO presence in fisheries domain indicates institutional support for fishery management

solutions: Fingerling stocking in tanks and perennial water bodies
Training of Community Resource Persons (CRPs) in fishery management, feeding, and harvesting
Fish marketing and value chain linkage to local and regional markets
Tank desilting to increase water holding capacity and fish habitat
Fishery rights formalisation through Self-Help Groups or cooperatives

economic_hardship.description: Households face economic distress, inability to meet basic needs, high debt, or livelihood loss.

multi_sector_vulnerability.description: Economic hardship is a cross-cutting outcome of failures across agriculture, livestock, forest, and fishery production systems combined with poor access to financial services.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Higher SC/ST proportion correlates with greater livelihood vulnerability and limited asset base
ST communities often depend on forests and commons which are degrading; loss compounds economic hardship
Low literacy limits access to government schemes, market information, and diversification opportunities
Remoteness from banking forces reliance on informal moneylenders; high debt is a primary hardship driver
High out-migration is both a symptom and cause of economic hardship and agricultural labour shortage
Direct measure of income adequacy and livelihood security
Recurring severe drought years cause crop losses that cascade into debt and hardship
MGNREGA works provide wage employment; low count indicates insufficient employment guarantee coverage
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).

solutions: Integrated watershed-livelihood programme combining SWC, agriculture, and livestock
MGNREGA saturation for wage employment during lean seasons
Convergence of government schemes (PM-KISAN, PMAY, NFSA) at village level
Financial inclusion: BC Sakhi, Jan Dhan, crop insurance enrolment drives
Community institutions (SHGs, FPOs) as platform for collective action

low_income.description: Household incomes are insufficient for basic needs; dependence on single livelihood source with no diversification.

small_landholding.description: Fragmented, small landholdings limit surplus production, mechanisation, and creditworthiness.

diagnostic_variables.rationale: Direct measure of landholding fragmentation; proportion of marginal (<1ha) and small (1–2ha) holders
Combined with MWS cropland area, gives a proxy for average farm size per household
Total cropland divided by village population gives approximate per-capita land availability
Higher cropping intensity increases effective production from small holdings; low intensity compounds income constraint
Small holders near APMCs have better price discovery; remote smallholders are exploited by intermediaries
Proximity to dairy infrastructure enables livestock income diversification for small holders
NGO presence in livelihoods, fisheries, or extension services indicates diversification support available
MGNREGA off-farm livelihood asset works provide supplementary income; cumulative count reflects safety net strength
Direct income measurement by source (farm / livestock / NTFP / wage / other) to diagnose livelihood mix
Price realisation gap vs MSP is a direct indicator of income loss from market failure
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).
Referenced in evidence-card signal expressions (derived or supporting indicator).

solutions: FPO formation for collective input procurement and output marketing
Lease-land farming models and cooperative farming on common land
Diversification into livestock, fishery, NTFP to supplement farm income
Skill development and non-farm livelihood promotion
Access to Kisan Credit Card and institutional credit for investment
